meta.title: Loka — Declarative UI in C++98
meta.description: Loka is a declarative GUI framework with a modern statically typed DSL built in C++98, projecting one UI model onto native toolkits from System 7 to modern Windows and macOS.

hero.title: Declarative UI in C++98
hero.subtitle: One declarative model, projected onto native toolkits — from System 7 and Mac OS X Tiger to modern Windows and macOS. Modern, explicit, and honest about ownership.

codeExample.heading: Modern shape, ancient toolchains
codeExample.body: Loka's DSL is plain C++98 with strong compile-time types — no exceptions, no RTTI, only a small STL surface. The same component builds with GCC 4.0-era compilers and today's Clang, GCC, and MSVC.

examples.heading: Examples
examples.body: Small, complete applications from the repository. Each one shares the same Node / Boundary / State / Flow model.

items.name: HelloWorld
items.description: A BMI calculator showing components, state, and text input.

items.name: MineSweeper
items.description: The classic game as a declarative grid of nodes.

items.name: FloppyBird
items.description: A small action game driving animation from the same model.

items.name: SimpleViewer
items.description: An image viewer using Flow for long-lived async work.

hardware.heading: Validated on real hardware
hardware.body: Loka treats retro platforms as first-class targets, not novelty ports. Changes are exercised on real machines — 68k and PowerPC Classic Macs, Tiger-era PowerPC laptops, and modern desktops — so the platform layers absorb real-world OS behavior, not emulator folklore.
hardware.imageAlt: Loka running on a lineup of vintage and modern laptops

gallery.name: VS Code
gallery.caption: Day-to-day development in VS Code with CMake and Ninja.

gallery.name: PowerBook G4
gallery.caption: Running the examples on a PowerBook G4 with Xcode 3.1.4.

items.title: A small, unified concept set
items.body: Node, Boundary, State, Props, and Flow are reused consistently across UI, events, async pipelines, and native integration — instead of a new mechanism for every feature.

items.title: Modern DSL, C++98 core
items.body: A statically typed declarative DSL that still builds on GCC 4.0-era toolchains, so 68k and PowerPC systems get the same programming model as modern desktops.

items.title: No exceptions, no RTTI
items.body: Core DSL paths avoid exceptions, RTTI, and most of the STL. Error handling is explicit and the dependency surface stays small enough for retro toolchains.

items.title: Deterministic lifecycle
items.body: Ownership, cleanup, and update routing are explicit behind the declarative API. State handles stay attached to a lifecycle owner — no hidden garbage collection.

items.title: One model, native projection
items.body: Logical UI design is separated from OS-specific projection. The core stays neutral while thin platform layers map the same structure onto native windowing APIs.

items.title: An executable Stack Overflow
items.body: Version-specific OS bugs and per-platform quirks usually survive only as folklore. Loka absorbs those fixes into its platform layers so app code can simply declare intent.

platforms.heading: Supported environments
platforms.body: Loka is designed around explicit target environments rather than assuming one modern desktop baseline.

items.name: Modern Windows / Win32
items.notes: Native Win32 projection path. Windows XP-class compatibility tracked as a legacy build target.

items.name: macOS / Cocoa
items.notes: Mac OS X 10.4 Tiger or newer, PowerPC G3 or newer.

items.name: Classic Mac OS / Toolbox
items.notes: Built through Retro68 for System 7 or later on 68k and PowerPC-class targets.

items.name: Linux / WSL
items.notes: Core and Flow DSL tests today. Native UI projection is planned, not part of 0.0.1.

items.name: iOS / iPadOS, Linux desktop UI, game-oriented backends
items.notes: Future ports reuse the same Node / Boundary / State / Flow model.

steps.title: Build and run the core tests
steps.body: A quick headless build on Linux, WSL, or macOS. You need CMake 3.19+, Ninja, and any C++98-capable compiler.
steps.code:
cmake -S . -B build/Testing -DTEST_BUILD=ON
cmake --build build/Testing
ctest --test-dir build/Testing

steps.title: Explore the examples
steps.body: Platform builds (macOS, Windows, Retro68) are documented in docs/environments.md. Start from example/HelloWorld and work up.
